doc.title: Treasury stock — cost method
doc.event: The company buys back 1,000 of its own shares at $20, then later reissues them at different prices. Under the cost method, treasury stock parks at what you paid — and reissuance differences flow through equity accounts, NEVER through income.

lines.label: 1,000 × $20 cost
lines.why: Cost method means exactly that: the treasury account holds what you PAID, ignoring par and original issue price entirely.
lines.trap: Treasury Stock is a CONTRA-EQUITY debit — not an asset. A company cannot own a piece of itself as an asset.

lines.label: the buyback price
lines.trap: No touch to Common Stock or PIC on the way in — under the cost method they wait untouched unless the shares are retired.

variants.label: Reissue 400 shares at $26 — above the $20 cost

lines.trap: Reissue price vs COST is the comparison — original issue price and par are spectators.

lines.label: 400 × $20 cost out
lines.why: Shares leave treasury at the same cost they entered — the account only ever moves at cost.
lines.trap: Crediting treasury at the reissue price strands a phantom balance in the account forever.

lines.label: the excess
lines.why: Selling above cost creates additional paid-in capital — from your own shareholders, but capital nonetheless.
lines.trap: NEVER 'Gain on Sale of Treasury Stock.' A company does not earn income trading its own shares — this credit is equity, full stop.

variants.label: Reissue 500 shares at $14 — below cost, the cascade fires

entries.caption: Reissue 500 treasury shares at $14 (deficiency $3,000; PIC-TS balance only $2,400)

lines.why: Proceeds below cost — a $3,000 shortfall against the $10,000 cost relieved.
lines.trap: The shortfall is not a loss on the income statement. It's absorbed inside equity, in a strict order.

lines.label: drain PIC-TS first
lines.why: First stop of the cascade: the PIC-TS built up from earlier above-cost reissues absorbs the deficiency — but only down to zero.
lines.trap: PIC-TS can never go negative. Debit only what the account actually holds ($2,400 here).

lines.label: the overflow
lines.why: Whatever PIC-TS can't absorb ($3,000 − $2,400) falls through to Retained Earnings — the cascade's last stop.
lines.trap: This RE debit is the most-tested treasury detail in the chapter. Skipping the cascade order (or sending the whole $3,000 to RE while PIC-TS sits full) both lose points.

lines.label: 500 × $20 cost out
lines.trap: Cost method's one constant: the treasury account moves at $20/share no matter what the market does.

computationPaths.narration: The below-cost cascade: deficiency = cost relieved − proceeds = 10,000 − 7,000 = 3,000. Absorb from PIC-TS first (2,400 available), then Retained Earnings takes the rest (600). Income statement: untouched.

memorize.body: Cost method in one line: Treasury Stock moves at COST both directions. Above-cost reissue → credit PIC-TS. Below-cost → debit PIC-TS to zero, overflow to Retained Earnings.
No gains, no losses, no income statement — ever. A company trading its own shares is rearranging equity, not earning.
Presentation: Treasury Stock is the LAST line of stockholders' equity, subtracted. Buybacks shrink both equity and shares outstanding (shares issued stays put).

questions.prompt: Treasury shares costing $10,000 are reissued for $7,000. PIC from Treasury Stock has a $2,400 balance. Retained Earnings is debited for…

distractors.feedback: The full deficiency skips the cascade's first stop — PIC-TS must absorb its $2,400 before RE takes anything. RE gets only the overflow: $600.
Zero would need PIC-TS to cover the whole $3,000 — it only holds $2,400. The $600 remainder must land in Retained Earnings (never in a loss account).

accountBank.account: Gain on Sale of Treasury Stock
Loss on Sale of Treasury Stock

build.scaffold: Anchor every leg on COST (what was paid going in). Reissue: cash at the new price, treasury out at cost, and the difference is equity plumbing — PIC-TS up when above, PIC-TS-then-RE down when below. The two gain/loss accounts in the bank are pure bait.
